text.headline: Project Timeline
text.text: This is a summary of both my code and life projects.

text.headline: Gittip
text.text:
Quit my wonderful job and became the first full-time employee at Gittip, one of the original "open companies". Starting at $7/week, the pay wasn't great, but there was a certain je-ne-sais-quoi. Can't imagine a more worthwhile way to burn through my Bitcoin savings.

view | code

text.headline: Open Source Sabbatical
text.text: Seeking a place where my financial runway could be extended, I broke my lease, threw a goodbye party, and headed for Nicaragua. I resolved not to return until my crowdfunding salary would cover living costs back in Canada. (Spoiler: This last bit didn't work out!)

text.headline: Mission Impossible Android
text.text:
When a Tor developer published a complex step-by-step tutorial on how to configure a secure phone, I figured we could make it a bit simpler. We worked to convert the tedious manual process into a secure Android ROM.

view | code

text.headline: Martus on Tails
text.text:
Martus is a secure database suite for journalists and NGO workers engaged in hostile jurisdictions. In cooperation with Nick Merrill, I put together a proof-of-concept process for distributing the secure Tails operating system with Martus pre-installed.

code

text.headline: TextNow Plus
text.text:
This system-level Android app aimed to allow standard SMS apps to send/receive text messages via digital SMS service providers (eg. Twilio, TextNow, Google Voice), without using special apps.

code

text.headline: Toronto Liquid Democracy Venture
text.text:
We are aspiring to create a real-world liquid democracy experience in Toronto. The goals and philosophies of this project is a lens through which the goals of later efforts come into focus.

view | code

text.headline: Urban Camping
text.text:
Living in Nicaragua gave me a taste for the good work I could get done when my costs were low. But my community was in Toronto, a very expensive city to live in. Frustrated with these tensions, I started looking for alternatives. I spent the summer months adapting my daily habits so that I didn't need an apartment.

I've now grown comfortable camping full-time in parks in SF & TO. This allows me to dedicate more time towards trying to create real value in the world, without worrying whether that value can immediately be transformed into money.

view

text.headline: Toronto City Hall API
text.text:
Built on open standards, this API allows anyone to easily build apps on data web-scraped from official city systems. It's the underlying plumbing on which Toronto Councilmatic depends.

view | code

text.headline: Civic Tech Toronto
text.text:
As a co-organizer, I help run weekly hacknights that bring together designers, coders, urban planners, government staff, mappers, policy-makers, students, communications strategists, and all other Torontonians who share an interest in making Toronto more responsive, prosperous, sustainable and equitable, through design, tech, and data.

view | code

text.headline: Toronto Councilmatic
text.text:
This project aims to create a beautiful citizen-run website that tracks all things related to City Council: the legislation introduced and passed, its various committees and the meetings they hold, and the councillors themselves.

view | code

text.headline: Citizen Data Portal
text.text:
This project aims to create a community-managed platform for cataloguing Toronto data. Uniquely, it collects all Toronto datasets in a single place, regardless of whether they originate from official city sources or from citizens themselves.

view | code

text.headline: Shipping Container Living
text.text:
A few friends were in NYC for a hacker conference, and happened to converge on a talk called Hacking Housing. It was hosted by some folks who'd created a scrappy incubator in Oakland, CA for building DIY "shipping container homes". As the talk ended, we each leaned forward and looked at one another with expressions of barely contained excitement. We committed then-and-there to regular meetings and working toward something similar in Toronto.

It's been a work in progress.

view

text.headline: Remote Deputation app
text.text:
Speak to Toronto City Council from anywhere, by submitting a short video.

No more long drives. No more inconvenient mid-day time slots. Feel free to wear your pajamas.

view | code

text.headline: MyDemØcracy.ca
text.text:
As part of the Canadian government's consultation on electoral reform, they created a survey at MyDemocracy.ca. The tool itself provoked a mixed response. Excited by another tool called Pol.is, a friend and I decided to launch a complementary effort: MyDemØcracy.ca

view | code

text.headline: Voter Registration Check API
text.text:
A custom API built on top of the official Elections Canada web form. This simple pass-through API allows developers to easily check whether a given citizen is verified to vote at a given address.

view | code

text.headline: National Digital Identity System
text.text: Aspiring to be a customized software fork of the US government's official Login.gov platform. It will be a high-assurance identity system for Canadians, achieved through postal mail verification of voting addresses. Third-party platforms will be able to use it for sign-in, though unlike Facebook, it can corroborate membership in geographic districts.

text.headline: Hypha Tech Worker Co-operative
text.text: We help organizations and communities redesign their relationships with digital technology. hypha.coop
